claims.text: A 'liminal space' is a transitional zone — physical or emotional — between two distinctly different states; common examples include airports at night, empty schools, and abandoned malls, which produce a recognizable feeling of unease and false-familiar nostalgia.

evidence.quote:
a liminal space
is defined as a place of transition a
threshold between two distinctly
different points signaling the end of
one and the beginning of another
limitable spaces do exist as physical
locations but they could also be an
emotional experience they occur during
periods of uncertainty and major life
changes events like a divorce or breakup
the death of a loved one the birth of a
child moving to a new city ending or
starting a new career all these create
liminality in our mind meaning our life
before this event is over and a new
period of life is about to begin
liminality is the unease and
apprehension we feel during this
transition liminal spaces in the real
world are a bit more difficult to Define
but you know one when you see one think
of an airport in the middle of the night
school during summer break a house just
after someone moves out or in this case
an accidentally discovered abandoned
shopping mall

claims.rationale: The host's definition aligns with how the term is used in the contemporary internet aesthetic / Liminal Spaces subreddit and broadly with the anthropological roots in Victor Turner. The 'unease + false familiarity' description is a fair summary of the lay usage.

claims.text: The Spanish TikTok creator 'Unico Sobreviviente' (Javier) claims to have woken up on February 13, 2027 in a Valencia hospital in an alternate universe where every other human on Earth has vanished, and posts videos of himself in deserted stadiums, shops and police stations as evidence.

evidence.quote:
Javier woke up
in a hospital in Valencia on February 13
2027 and claims to be in an alternative
Universe apparently at some point in the
near future every human on earth just
disappears he said that when he woke up
he couldn't remember his name or where
he lived he went outside and everyone
was just gone everything appeared just
like 2021 but electronic devices showed
2027

claims.rationale: The Unico Sobreviviente account is real and posts videos of empty Valencia locations exactly as described. The factual claim that Javier posts these videos and presents this storyline is true; the metaphysical claim (he is actually in 2027 in an alternate universe) is later debunked by the host himself in claim 7.

claims.text: The Backrooms began as a single anonymous 4chan creepypasta in 2019 — a photo of a yellow office hallway with a caption about 'no-clipping out of reality' — and grew into a fully crowdsourced lore with multiple wikis, hundreds of levels, and its own canon and entities.

evidence.quote:
is an internet mystery that began like
many internet Mysteries do on 4chan
someone asked members to submit
disquieting images that just feel off an
anonymous user posted this Photograph
everyone who saw the image agreed it was
strangely familiar but unsettling though
nobody could explain why finally a
follow-up comment described it if you're
not careful and you know clip out of
reality in the wrong areas you'll end up
in the back
or it's nothing but the stink of old
moist carpet the madness mono yellow the
endless background noise of fluorescent
lights and maximum humbas and
approximately 600 million square miles
of randomly segmented empty rooms to be
trapped in God save you if you hear
something wandering around nearby
because it sure as hell has heard you uh
no clip well no clip is a video game
term when two objects overlap that's
clipping no clipping means crossing
boundaries in a game that you're not
supposed to cross and ending up an area
is not meant for the player the back
room's Theory says it's possible to no
clip into a different reality adjacent
to ours a reality you're not meant to
ever see remember Limitless spaces are
transitional you're not supposed to stay
there very long you instinctively want
to get out to move on the back rooms are
unnerving because you can't it's an
endless liminal space with no Escape
something about the back rooms connected
with people and an entire culture was
organically developed around the concept
completely Community Driven

claims.rationale: The Backrooms originated on 4chan's /x/ board on May 12, 2019 with the now-iconic yellow-hallway image and the 'if you're not careful, you no-clip out of reality' caption. The proliferation across the Backrooms Wiki and the Fandom wiki with multiple parallel canons is well documented. The basic origin story is correct.

claims.text: Teenage filmmaker Kane Parsons (aka Kane Pixels) brought the Backrooms into the mainstream with his viral 'Found Footage' YouTube series, beginning January 2022, which combines live action and 3D animation framed as a 1996 student film whose camera operator no-clips into the Backrooms.

evidence.quote:
a series of short videos
by a Creator named Kane Parsons using an
ingenious combination of live action and
3D animation wrapped in a low-tech
package these videos give us a glimpse
of what being trapped in the back rooms
would feel like the videos also prove
that liminal spaces are not a fringe
Theory millions of people have watched
these clearly the concept of liminality
is universal the video series begins
with a group of kids shooting what looks
like a student film in 1996 the camera
operator suddenly no clips through the
ground and and falls into the back rooms

claims.rationale: Kane Parsons (b. 2006) is real, his Kane Pixels YouTube channel is real, and his original Backrooms 'Found Footage' video posted Jan 7, 2022 with the 1996 student-film framing is well known and verifiably has hundreds of millions of views. A24 also signed Parsons to direct a feature adaptation in 2023, lending corroboration.

claims.text: The Backrooms aesthetic taps into a feeling of being trapped in an infinite, time-less, fluorescent office space — the abstract horror of being unable to find an exit from a transitional space — and this resonance explains why the concept exploded in popularity during COVID-19 lockdowns.

evidence.quote:
for anyone who's worked in an
office like this there's a feeling of
claustrophobia of being trapped like a
prisoner where time seems to slow down
and hours feel like they last for days
unfortunately for people trapped in the
back rooms time doesn't exist at all
there is no past and no future so no
brief respite for lunch no exhilaration
at the end of a long boring day the only
time is the present and like the space
itself the present is infinite

its popularity surged
during coveted lockdowns when everyone
was feeling isolated

claims.rationale: The pandemic-isolation thesis is widely repeated in cultural commentary about why liminal-space content went viral 2020-2022, and is supported by Google Trends data showing 'liminal space' search interest surging from May 2020. It is plausible and well-supported but ultimately a cultural-causation argument rather than a clean empirical claim.

claims.text: The concept of 'liminality' was coined by anthropologist Arnold van Gennep in his 1908 book Rites of Passage to describe transitional life stages like coming-of-age, before being elaborated by Victor Turner and adopted by horror media (The Shining, It Follows, Silent Hill, Severance) and later the internet aesthetic.

evidence.quote:
the phrase was
created by Arnold van gennep in his book
Rites of Passage released in 1908 and
getup was more focused on life
experiences like transitioning from
childhood to adulthood graduating high
school or moving to a new city this
concept of an in-between place whether
physical or emotional has been explored
in media and pop culture for years Rod
Serling famously said it is the middle
ground between light and Shadow between
science and Superstition and it lies
between the pit of Man's fears and the
summit of his knowledge The Shining is
an entire movie based around the idea of
liminality the abandoned Hotel spooky
hallways that go on forever and you
never know what lurks around Each corner
thank you
[Music]
horror movies like it follows and Silent
Hill also used liminal spaces to amp up
the creepiness and recently the Apple TV
sci-fi thriller Severance uses liminal
spaces as a backdrop for the entire
series the large empty spaces hallways
that create such a confusing maze that
you have to draw maps to find your way
around and the Dual reality concept of
severance is similar to The Back rooms

claims.rationale: Arnold van Gennep's Les rites de passage was published in 1909 (often cited as 1908 — the host is in the right ballpark). He did coin 'liminal' in this book; Victor Turner's later elaboration in the 1960s is what made the term central in anthropology. The horror-media examples cited all do explicitly use liminal-space aesthetics. The intellectual history is essentially correct.

claims.text: Javier's 'Lonely Survivor' deserted-world TikTok is not a paranormal account but a media project produced in collaboration with the city of Valencia, which is why the creator has filming permits and physical access to closed-off hospitals, stadiums, and police facilities.

evidence.quote:
Javier is working with the city of
Valencia on a media project called The
Lonely Survivor which is why he has
access to all these places and why he's
able to clear out entire stadiums busy
streets even sections of hospitals so
it's not some future reality it's just a
TV production team with filming permits

claims.rationale: Javier Pérez (account @unicosobreviviente) does collaborate with Valencia city tourism / cultural projects under 'El Último Habitante' / lonely-survivor framings, with filming permits being the prosaic explanation for his access. The host's debunking is consistent with reporting on the account.

dependencies.rationale: This claim is the host's debunking of the supernatural framing in c_0002.

claims.text: The viral Airbnb 'door to an abandoned mall' video has a banal explanation: the woman's Airbnb is a hotel that shares a building with the half-empty Ocean Walk Mall in Hollywood Beach, Florida — one of America's many 'dead malls' where physical retail has collapsed under online shopping.

evidence.quote:
her Airbnb is actually a hotel that
shares a building with the Ocean Walk
Mall in Hollywood Beach Florida an Ocean
Walk is what's called a dead Mall though
a better name for a dead mall is
probably a zombie mall it's a shopping
mall that nobody really goes to anymore
but it's still open and somewhat
functioning and dead malls are
everywhere all over the country and it's
not hard to understand why malls are
dying Amazon well pretty much online
shopping

claims.rationale: The Ocean Walk Mall in Hollywood Beach, FL is real and is widely catalogued as a dead-mall example, and the adjacent Marriott / Margaritaville-style hotel does share its building. Multiple debunkings of the Airbnb-mall-door TikTok identify exactly this location. The 'dead mall' phenomenon driven by online retail is a well documented retail trend.

claims.text: Liminal-space content's emotional power lies in its inversion of the standard narrative: every transition is meant to end, and the horror of the Backrooms / deserted-world genre is being permanently stuck in the in-between rather than passing through it.

evidence.quote:
every liminal space has the same
point
to find your way out

claims.rationale: This is the host's thematic interpretation rather than an empirical claim — but as an interpretation it is well-aligned with how cultural critics have described the genre's appeal. It's a defensible thesis statement, not a falsifiable empirical claim.
